subject: Science

unit.unitOverview: Look around you right now. A piece of charcoal glows red under the pot as your mother cooks bariis (rice). A steel gate on the coast near Berbera is slowly turning orange with rust. A cup of warm milk left overnight has thickened into yoghurt. A slice of mango turns brown after you cut it. Every one of these is a change to a material - the substance you started with has become something new, or has simply changed its form. In this unit you will learn to tell these two kinds of change apart and to explain, like a real scientist, exactly what is happening.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, experiments and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured directly from the Stage 7 science workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: By the end of this unit you will be able to:
Describe the difference between a physical change and a chemical change, with your own examples.
Explain what happens to substances in a chemical reaction, using the words reactant and product.
Recognise the signs that tell you a chemical reaction has taken place (gas, colour change, heat, a new solid, a change in pH, a substance being used up).
Explain burning (combustion) as a reaction between a fuel and the oxygen in the air.
Describe oxidation and rusting, and explain why iron rusts faster in damp, salty coastal air.
Explain what happens when an acid reacts with an alkali, and describe neutralisation as a change to pH 7.
Give real-life examples of neutralisation, such as antacids for a sour stomach, toothpaste, and lime added to acidic soil.
State the law of conservation of mass - that no atoms are lost or made in a reaction.
Carry out safe household investigations and record your observations like a scientist.

concepts.title: Scientists sort all changes into two families. In a…
concepts.explanation: Scientists sort all changes into two families. In a physical change, no new substance is made - the material only changes its shape, its size, or its state (solid, liquid or gas). When water freezes into ice or melts back again, it is still water. In a chemical change (also called a chemical reaction), one or more brand-new substances are formed, and these new substances have different properties from the ones you started with. When wood burns to ash, the ash can never turn back into wood. That is the big idea of…
concepts.example: This knowledge is not just for a classroom. It explains why the iron roofs and nails on the humid Indian Ocean coast rust so much faster than in the dry inland savanna, why a spoon of bicarbonate of soda can calm a…

concepts.title: This knowledge is not just for a classroom. It explains why…
concepts.explanation: This knowledge is not just for a classroom. It explains why the iron roofs and nails on the humid Indian Ocean coast rust so much faster than in the dry inland savanna, why a spoon of bicarbonate of soda can calm a sour, burning stomach, why baking soda and lemon or vinegar fizz up when they meet, why charcoal must have air to keep burning, and why milk turns into yoghurt. When you understand chemical changes, you understand cooking, cleaning, rusting, burning, digestion and much of the living world. As Muslims we…

explorations.title: The Fizzing Gas Test (Vinegar + Bicarbonate of Soda)
explorations.context: To show that a chemical reaction between vinegar and bicarbonate of soda makes a gas, and to test that the gas is carbon dioxide.
explorations.prompt: Before you start, write down what you think will happen when the two are mixed, and whether you think this is a physical or a chemical change. Give a reason.
explorations.answer: What evidence tells you a chemical reaction took place? Give at least two signs. The gas made is carbon dioxide. Carbon dioxide does not let things burn. How did the candle flame prove this? Name the acid and the alkali you used (the reactants). Was this a physical or a chemical change? Explain how…
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To show that a chemical reaction between vinegar and bicarbonate of soda makes a gas, and to test that the gas is carbon dioxide.

explorations.title: Rusting Nails (What Does Iron Need to Rust?)
explorations.context: To find out whether iron needs water, air, or both in order to rust.
explorations.prompt: Predict which nail(s) will rust the most and which will not rust at all. Explain your thinking.
explorations.answer: Which jar showed the most rust? Which showed none? What two things must be present together for iron to rust? If you tried Jar D, did salt make the rusting faster or slower? How does this explain coastal rust? Is rusting a physical or a chemical change? Give a reason.
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To find out whether iron needs water, air, or both in order to rust.

explorations.title: Make Your Own Indicator (Red Cabbage or Hibiscus)
explorations.context: To make a natural indicator and use it to sort household liquids into acids and alkalis.
explorations.prompt: Predict which of your test liquids are acids and which are alkalis before you test them.
explorations.answer: Which colour showed an acid? Which colour showed an alkali? Which liquid was closest to neutral? Now for neutralisation: add a little bicarbonate of soda a pinch at a time to a cup of lemon juice with indicator in it. Describe how the colour changes as it moves towards neutral. Why is a home-made…
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To make a natural indicator and use it to sort household liquids into acids and alkalis.

explorations.title: Physical or Chemical? A Kitchen Sorting Investigation
explorations.context: To observe several everyday kitchen changes and decide whether each is physical or chemical, using the signs of a reaction.
explorations.prompt: Predict which of the changes below will be chemical and which will be physical.
explorations.answer: Which changes made a new substance? How could you tell? Which changes could be reversed to get the starting material back? Which sign of a chemical reaction did you see in the browning fruit? Explain why toasting bread or souring milk is a chemical change but melting ice is not.
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To observe several everyday kitchen changes and decide whether each is physical or chemical, using the signs of a reaction.

methods.title: The Fizzing Gas Test (Vinegar + Bicarbonate of Soda)
methods.example: To show that a chemical reaction between vinegar and bicarbonate of soda makes a gas, and to test that the gas is carbon dioxide.
steps: Put two teaspoons of bicarbonate of soda into the bottom of the tall glass.
Pour in about half a cup of vinegar and watch closely. Record what you see and hear.
Feel the outside of the glass gently. Is it warmer, cooler, or the same?

For the gas test, ask an adult to light a small candle. Let the fizzing settle a little, then carefully tip the glass (do not pour out the liquid) so the invisible gas above the liquid pours over the candle flame.
Watch what happens to the flame. Record your observation.

methods.title: Rusting Nails (What Does Iron Need to Rust?)
methods.example: To find out whether iron needs water, air, or both in order to rust.
steps: Jar A: place a nail in and add enough water to half-cover it, leaving the top of the nail in the air.
Jar B: place a nail in and keep it completely dry; seal the jar so damp air is kept out.
Jar C: place a nail in and cover it fully with cooking oil so no air can reach it.
Optional Jar D: like Jar A but stir a spoon of salt into the water (like coastal sea air).
Label the jars and leave them somewhere safe for 4 to 6 days.

methods.title: Make Your Own Indicator (Red Cabbage or Hibiscus)
methods.example: To make a natural indicator and use it to sort household liquids into acids and alkalis.
steps: With an adult's help, pour hot water over the chopped red cabbage or hibiscus and leave it until the water is deeply coloured. Let it cool, then strain out the solids. This coloured liquid is your indicator.
Pour a little indicator into each clear cup.
Add a spoon of a different test liquid to each cup and stir gently.
Record the colour in each cup and decide if the liquid is acid, neutral, or alkali.
Observation Recording Sheet

methods.title: Physical or Chemical? A Kitchen Sorting Investigation
methods.example: To observe several everyday kitchen changes and decide whether each is physical or chemical, using the signs of a reaction.
steps: Change 1: stir sugar into warm water until it disappears. Taste a tiny drop (this one is safe - it is food) to check the sugar is still there.
Change 2: let an ice cube melt in a cup.
Change 3: leave a cut slice of fruit exposed to the air for an hour and watch the surface.
Change 4: with an adult, lightly toast a small piece of bread, OR leave a spoon of milk in a warm place for a day to sour.
Record each change and look for signs of a reaction.

workedExamples.title: Worked example
workedExamples.solution: Problem: Amina heats 24 grams of magnesium in the air until it fully burns. It combines with 16 grams of oxygen from the air to make magnesium oxide. What mass of magnesium oxide is formed? Step 1 - Name the reactants and product. Reactants: magnesium (24 g) and oxygen (16 g). Product: magnesium oxide. Step 2 - Apply conservation of mass. Total mass of products equals total mass of reactants, because atoms are only rearranged. Step 3 - Add the reactant masses. 24 g + 16 g = 40 g.

workedExamples.title: 1 - Conservation of Mass
workedExamples.prompt: Problem: Amina heats 24 grams of magnesium in the air until it fully burns. It combines with 16 grams of oxygen from the air to make magnesium oxide. What mass of magnesium oxide is formed?
workedExamples.solution: Step 1 - Name the reactants and product. Reactants: magnesium (24 g) and oxygen (16 g). Product: magnesium oxide. Step 2 - Apply conservation of mass. Total mass of products equals total mass of reactants, because atoms are only rearranged. Step 3 - Add the reactant masses. 24 g + 16 g = 40 g. Answer: 40 grams of magnesium oxide is formed. Notice that the product is heavier than the magnesium alone, because oxygen atoms from the air have joined on.

workedExamples.title: 2 - Reading a Neutralisation Graph
workedExamples.prompt: Problem: Khadija puts 50 cm3 of an alkali in a cup and adds acid slowly, checking the pH after each 10 cm3. She records: 0 cm3 -> pH 12; 10 -> 11; 20 -> 10; 30 -> 9; 40 -> 8; 50 -> 7. How much acid did she need to make the solution neutral, and what is…
workedExamples.solution: Step 1 - Recall what neutral means. A neutral solution has a pH of exactly 7. Step 2 - Find pH 7 in the table. The pH reaches 7 after 50 cm3 of acid has been added. Step 3 - Interpret the trend. As acid is added, the pH falls steadily from 12 towards 7. The acid is neutralising the alkali little by little. Answer: She needed 50 cm3 of acid to neutralise the alkali. If she added even more acid, the pH would drop below 7 and the mixture would become acidic.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 4
workedExamples.prompt: When charcoal (carbon) burns in air it makes: (a) hydrogen (b) water only (c) carbon dioxide (d) magnesium oxide
workedExamples.solution: 4 (c) carbon dioxide Carbon combines with oxygen from the air to make carbon dioxide gas.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 5
workedExamples.prompt: Iron rusts when it is exposed to: (a) oxygen only (b) water only (c) both oxygen and water (d) neither
workedExamples.solution: 5 (c) both oxygen and water Iron needs oxygen AND water together to rust.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 6
workedExamples.prompt: Rusting is an example of: (a) melting (b) slow oxidation (c) dissolving (d) neutralisation
workedExamples.solution: 6 (b) slow oxidation Rusting is iron combining slowly with oxygen - oxidation without a flame.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 7
workedExamples.prompt: The pH of a neutral solution is: (a) 0 (b) 3 (c) 7 (d) 14
workedExamples.solution: 7 (c) 7 Neutral is exactly pH 7, midway on the 0-14 scale.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 8
workedExamples.prompt: An antacid used for a sour stomach is: (a) an acid (b) an alkali (c) neutral (d) a metal
workedExamples.solution: 8 (b) an alkali Antacids are alkalis that neutralise excess stomach acid.

practice.prompt: Which of these is a physical change? (a) burning charcoal (b) melting butter (c) a nail rusting (d) milk turning to yoghurt
practice.answer: 1 (b) melting butter Melting changes state only; no new substance is made, so it is physical.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: In a chemical reaction, the new substances made are called the: (a) reactants (b) products (c) mixtures (d) elements
practice.answer: 2 (b) products Products are the new substances made; reactants are what you start with.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which is a sign that a chemical reaction has happened? (a) a substance changes shape (b) a gas is given off (c) ice melts (d) sugar dissolves
practice.answer: 3 (b) a gas is given off The others (shape change, melting, dissolving) are physical changes.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: When charcoal (carbon) burns in air it makes: (a) hydrogen (b) water only (c) carbon dioxide (d) magnesium oxide
practice.answer: 4 (c) carbon dioxide Carbon combines with oxygen from the air to make carbon dioxide gas.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Iron rusts when it is exposed to: (a) oxygen only (b) water only (c) both oxygen and water (d) neither
practice.answer: 5 (c) both oxygen and water Iron needs oxygen AND water together to rust.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Rusting is an example of: (a) melting (b) slow oxidation (c) dissolving (d) neutralisation
practice.answer: 6 (b) slow oxidation Rusting is iron combining slowly with oxygen - oxidation without a flame.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: The pH of a neutral solution is: (a) 0 (b) 3 (c) 7 (d) 14
practice.answer: 7 (c) 7 Neutral is exactly pH 7, midway on the 0-14 scale.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: An antacid used for a sour stomach is: (a) an acid (b) an alkali (c) neutral (d) a metal
practice.answer: 8 (b) an alkali Antacids are alkalis that neutralise excess stomach acid.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which gas relights a glowing splint? (a) hydrogen (b) carbon dioxide (c) oxygen (d) nitrogen
practice.answer: 9 (c) oxygen Oxygen relights a glowing splint; this is the test for oxygen.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: When an acid reacts with an alkali the reaction is called: (a) combustion (b) neutralisation (c) rusting (d) melting
practice.answer: 10 (b) neutralisation Acid + alkali reacting to make a neutral solution is neutralisation.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: The law of conservation of mass says that in a reaction the total mass of products is: (a) less than the reactants (b) more than the reactants (c) equal to the reactants (d) always zero
practice.answer: 11 (c) equal to the reactants Atoms are only rearranged, so total mass stays the same.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which household pair fizzes because a gas is made? (a) sugar and water (b) oil and water (c) vinegar and bicarbonate of soda (d) salt and water
practice.answer: 12 (c) vinegar and bicarbonate of soda The acid and alkali react to make carbon dioxide gas.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

activities.title: The Fizzing Gas Test (Vinegar + Bicarbonate of Soda)
activities.materials: White vinegar or lemon juice (an acid); Bicarbonate of soda / baking soda (an alkali); A tall glass or a small clean bottle; A spoon and a teaspoon; A small candle in a holder and an adult to light it (for the gas test); A jug or bowl to work over
steps: Put two teaspoons of bicarbonate of soda into the bottom of the tall glass.
Pour in about half a cup of vinegar and watch closely. Record what you see and hear.
Feel the outside of the glass gently. Is it warmer, cooler, or the same?

For the gas test, ask an adult to light a small candle. Let the fizzing settle a little, then carefully tip the glass (do not pour out the liquid) so the invisible gas above the liquid pours over the candle flame.
Watch what happens to the flame. Record your observation.

activities.title: Rusting Nails (What Does Iron Need to Rust?)
activities.materials: Three clean iron or steel nails (not stainless steel or galvanised); Three clear jars or glasses; Water; salt; cooking oil; A little cotton wool or a tight lid for the dry jar
steps: Jar A: place a nail in and add enough water to half-cover it, leaving the top of the nail in the air.
Jar B: place a nail in and keep it completely dry; seal the jar so damp air is kept out.
Jar C: place a nail in and cover it fully with cooking oil so no air can reach it.
Optional Jar D: like Jar A but stir a spoon of salt into the water (like coastal sea air).
Label the jars and leave them somewhere safe for 4 to 6 days.

activities.title: Make Your Own Indicator (Red Cabbage or Hibiscus)
activities.materials: A few leaves of purple red cabbage, OR a handful of dried dark hibiscus flowers; Hot water and an adult to help with it, plus a strainer; Several clear cups; Test liquids: lemon juice or vinegar (acid), water (neutral), bicarbonate of soda dissolved in water and soapy water (alkali)
steps: With an adult's help, pour hot water over the chopped red cabbage or hibiscus and leave it until the water is deeply coloured. Let it cool, then strain out the solids. This coloured liquid is your indicator.
Pour a little indicator into each clear cup.
Add a spoon of a different test liquid to each cup and stir gently.
Record the colour in each cup and decide if the liquid is acid, neutral, or alkali.
Observation Recording Sheet

activities.title: Physical or Chemical? A Kitchen Sorting Investigation
activities.materials: A little sugar and a cup of warm water; an ice cube; A slice of apple, mango or banana; A small piece of bread (to toast with an adult) or a spoon of milk left to warm and sour; A clean cup and spoon
steps: Change 1: stir sugar into warm water until it disappears. Taste a tiny drop (this one is safe - it is food) to check the sugar is still there.
Change 2: let an ice cube melt in a cup.
Change 3: leave a cut slice of fruit exposed to the air for an hour and watch the surface.
Change 4: with an adult, lightly toast a small piece of bread, OR leave a spoon of milk in a warm place for a day to sour.
Record each change and look for signs of a reaction.

terms: A change with no new substance made; only shape, size or state changes; often reversible.
A change that makes one or more new substances with new properties; usually not reversible.
A substance you start with in a reaction.
A new substance made in a reaction.
To join together to make a new substance.
A reaction of a fuel with oxygen that releases heat and light.
A reaction in which a substance combines with oxygen.
Iron oxide - the orange-brown solid made when iron meets oxygen and water.
A substance with pH above 7; the opposite of an acid.
An acid reacting with an alkali to make a neutral solution (pH 7).
commonMistakes: Calling melting or dissolving a chemical change.
The ash weighs less than the wood.
Thinking a rusting nail loses mass.

realProblems.prompt: Sagal's family in Mombasa finds their metal gate rusts within a year, but her cousin's gate in the dry Rift Valley lasts much longer. Explain the difference and suggest two ways Sagal's family could protect their gate.
realProblems.answer: Mombasa's coastal air is warm, damp and salty, giving iron plenty of water and oxygen (with salt speeding it up), so it rusts fast; the Rift Valley air is drier. Protection: paint the gate or coat it with oil/grease (or use galvanised metal) to keep water and oxygen away from the iron.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Mombasa's coastal air is warm, damp and salty, giving iron plenty of water and oxygen (with salt speeding it up), so it rusts fast; the Rift Valley air is drier. Protection: paint the gate or coat it with oil/grease (or use galvanised metal) to keep water and oxygen away from the iron.

realProblems.prompt: Omar has a burning, sour stomach after a large meal. His mother gives him a spoon of bicarbonate of soda in water. Explain, using the words acid and alkali, why this helps.
realProblems.answer: Stomach acid is an acid; bicarbonate of soda is an alkali. They neutralise each other, raising the pH towards 7 and easing the burning feeling.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Stomach acid is an acid; bicarbonate of soda is an alkali. They neutralise each other, raising the pH towards 7 and easing the burning feeling.

realProblems.prompt: A farmer near the Jubba River finds his sorghum grows poorly because the soil is too acidic. What could he add to the soil, and what is this reaction called?
realProblems.answer: He should add lime (an alkali) to neutralise the acidic soil, raising the pH so crops grow better. The reaction is neutralisation.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: He should add lime (an alkali) to neutralise the acidic soil, raising the pH so crops grow better. The reaction is neutralisation.

realProblems.prompt: Hani mixes vinegar and bicarbonate of soda to clean a pot and sees it fizz. Name the gas made and state two signs that told her a chemical reaction happened.
realProblems.answer: The gas is carbon dioxide. Two signs: bubbles/fizzing (a gas given off) and it happening without heating - plus you cannot easily reverse it. (A cool feeling is also acceptable.)
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: The gas is carbon dioxide. Two signs: bubbles/fizzing (a gas given off) and it happening without heating - plus you cannot easily reverse it. (A cool feeling is also acceptable.)

realProblems.prompt: Explain why leaving fresh camel milk in a warm place turns it into a new substance, and name the kind of change.
realProblems.answer: Bacteria turn the milk sugar into acid, which makes the milk proteins clump into yoghurt/soured milk - a new substance, so it is a chemical change.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Bacteria turn the milk sugar into acid, which makes the milk proteins clump into yoghurt/soured milk - a new substance, so it is a chemical change.

realProblems.prompt: A piece of magnesium has a mass of 12 g. It burns and combines with 8 g of oxygen. What is the mass of magnesium oxide formed? Which law did you use, and explain it in one sentence.
realProblems.answer: 12 g + 8 g = 20 g of magnesium oxide. Law used: conservation of mass - atoms are only rearranged, so the total mass of the products equals the total mass of the reactants.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 12 g + 8 g = 20 g of magnesium oxide. Law used: conservation of mass - atoms are only rearranged, so the total mass of the products equals the total mass of the reactants.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: In one sentence each, define a physical change and a chemical change.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Physical change: a change where no new substance is made and it can often be reversed (e.g. melting). Chemical change: a change where a new substance is formed with different properties (e.g. burning).

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Name the reactants and the product when magnesium burns in air.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Reactants: magnesium and oxygen. Product: magnesium oxide. Magnesium combines with oxygen from the air.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: List the six signs that a chemical reaction has taken place.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Gas given off; colour change; heat or light given off (or taken in); a new solid (precipitate) forms; a change in pH; a reactant is used up. Any spotted sign is strong evidence of a reaction.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain why a fire goes out if you cover it so no air can reach it.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Burning needs oxygen from the air. If air is cut off, the oxygen runs out and the reaction stops, so the fire goes out.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Describe the test for hydrogen gas and the test for carbon dioxide gas.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Hydrogen: a lighted splint makes it burn with a squeaky pop. Carbon dioxide: it turns limewater cloudy (and puts out a flame).

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain why iron rusts faster near the sea at Berbera than inland at Garissa.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: The coast has warm, damp, salty air - plenty of water and oxygen, sped up by salt - while Garissa's air is dry, so much less rusting.

questions.question: Which of these is a physical change?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 8 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: melting butter Melting changes state only; no new substance is made, so it is physical.

questions.question: In a chemical reaction, the new substances made are called the:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 8 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: products Products are the new substances made; reactants are what you start with.

questions.question: Which is a sign that a chemical reaction has happened?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 8 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: a gas is given off The others (shape change, melting, dissolving) are physical changes.

questions.question: When charcoal (carbon) burns in air it makes:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 8 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: carbon dioxide Carbon combines with oxygen from the air to make carbon dioxide gas.

questions.question: Iron rusts when it is exposed to:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 8 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: both oxygen and water Iron needs oxygen AND water together to rust.

questions.question: Rusting is an example of:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 8 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: slow oxidation Rusting is iron combining slowly with oxygen - oxidation without a flame.

questions.question: The pH of a neutral solution is:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 8 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: 7 Neutral is exactly pH 7, midway on the 0-14 scale.

questions.question: An antacid used for a sour stomach is:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 8 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: an alkali Antacids are alkalis that neutralise excess stomach acid.

questions.question: Which gas relights a glowing splint?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 8 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: oxygen Oxygen relights a glowing splint; this is the test for oxygen.

questions.question: When an acid reacts with an alkali the reaction is called:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 8 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: neutralisation Acid + alkali reacting to make a neutral solution is neutralisation.

questions.question: The law of conservation of mass says that in a reaction the total mass of products is:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 8 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: equal to the reactants Atoms are only rearranged, so total mass stays the same.

questions.question: Which household pair fizzes because a gas is made?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 8 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: vinegar and bicarbonate of soda The acid and alkali react to make carbon dioxide gas.

selfAssessment: I can by the end of this unit you will be able to:
I can describe the difference between a physical change and a chemical change, with your own examples.
I can explain what happens to substances in a chemical reaction, using the words reactant and product.
I can recognise the signs that tell you a chemical reaction has taken place (gas, colour change, heat, a new solid, a change in pH, a substance being used up).
I can explain burning (combustion) as a reaction between a fuel and the oxygen in the air.
I can describe oxidation and rusting, and explain why iron rusts faster in damp, salty coastal air.
I can explain what happens when an acid reacts with an alkali, and describe neutralisation as a change to pH 7.
I can give real-life examples of neutralisation, such as antacids for a sour stomach, toothpaste, and lime added to acidic soil.
